summary: electron-orbit: postinstall harvests installer env vars and host identity; import-time hand-rolled TLS exfiltration
details: Package impersonates the OrbitDB project (author 'OrbitDB Archive', homepage pointing at the orbitdb-archive GitHub org) while shipping a two-stage installer-data exfiltration chain. The postinstall hook (package.json line 47: "postinstall": "node install.js") runs install.js, which enumerates process.env (filtering out PATH-keyed entries), os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, cwd, and runtime metadata and writes them as JSON to a staging file under os.tmpdir() (electron_orbit_install_marker.txt). The strings 'process', 'env', and the marker filename are XOR-decoded at runtime via an __s(key, bytes) helper to evade static analysis. On require()/import, dist/index.js reads back this marker, XOR-encodes the contents with the literal 'electron-orbit', hex-encodes them, and dist/aetherls.js opens a raw TLS 1.3 session built by hand over node:net.connect(host, 443) (rather than the standard https module) to send a GET request whose 'GET ', ' HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: ', 'node:net', 'connect', and ALPN 'http/1.1' literals are all XOR-hidden. The destination host is selected from a 'registeredIconSources' table gated by a sha256(process.env.BuildType) substring comparison against a hardcoded digest, allowing the operator to swap in the live target via an environment variable while the published artifact ships placeholder URLs. The README advertises only a benign 'runtime discovery toolkit' and does not disclose env collection or outbound networking. Hand-rolling TLS to bypass proxy/MITM inspection, XOR-obfuscating exactly the network and process primitives used for collection and exfiltration, and hash-gating the live destination together establish purpose-built supply-chain malware.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
